- Summary
- Companies

- Fertiliser supplies cut off by Middle East war
- Farmers worse off than during 2022 fertiliser crunch
- Analysts fear planting and yield drag as war disruption persists

PARIS/SINGAPORE, April 27 (Reuters) - Farmers around the world are facing the second surge in fertiliser prices in four years due to the [Iran war](https://www.reuters.com/world/iran/). But with grain prices too low to cushion the blow from the deeper supply crunch this time around, many are rethinking planting plans, putting global food ​production at risk.

The Middle East is a leading fertiliser production hub, and much of the global fertiliser trade typically passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which has seen traffic brought to a ‌standstill by the conflict.

The Reuters Iran Briefing newsletter keeps you informed with the latest developments and analysis of the Iran war. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-iran-briefing/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fworld%2Fchina%2Firan-war-fertiliser-squeeze-could-spell-trouble-next-years-grain-harvests-2026-04-27%2F)

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Supplies of urea - a nitrogen-based fertiliser - from the world's largest production facility in Qatar have been halted, and flows of sulphur and ammonia, common inputs for a range of fertilisers, have also been curbed.

With a resolution of the conflict proving elusive, analysts, traders, fertiliser producers and agronomists are looking back at the last supply crisis, Russia's 2022 [invasion of Ukraine](https://www.reuters.com/world/ukraine-russia-war/), worried that this time things could get even worse.

"Back in 2022, a lot of the fertiliser was ultimately flowing through," said Shawn Arita of the Agricultural Risk Policy Center ​at North Dakota State University.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

"It's a much steeper supply crunch that we're seeing now."

## CASH-STRAPPED FARMERS GRAPPLE WITH PRICE SPIKE

As [fertiliser prices](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/how-does-iran-war-affect-fertiliser-supplies-prices-food-security-2026-03-17/) have jumped since the onset of the war in late February, urea has seen the ​sharpest price spike, reflecting the loss of the roughly one-third of globally traded volumes typically exported from the Gulf.

A line chart with the title 'Urea prices'

Some are paying. India, the world's largest rice producer and ⁠second-biggest wheat grower, has booked record volumes of urea in a single import tender, [paying nearly twice as much as it did just two months ago](https://www.reuters.com/world/india/india-import-record-25-million-tons-urea-nearly-double-price-paid-two-months-ago-2026-04-22/).

But such price levels are beyond the reach of many, analysts say.

In 2022, high global ​grain prices helped farmers offset the steep increase in input costs caused by the Ukraine war. But ample harvests of grains and oilseeds in recent years have restrained crop prices.

Chicago wheat prices are roughly half what they were four ​years ago, for example. Soybeans were nearly 50% higher than now.

As a result, many growers today lack the revenue to absorb ballooning fertiliser bills.

Chicago wheat and soybean prices since 2022

Nitrogen-based fertilisers such as urea must be applied each season for many crops and directly influence annual yields as well as quality parameters, including protein content in wheat.

Farmers can cut back on other core nutrients, such as phosphate and potash, without immediate yield losses.

Even that option may be tested, however, if phosphate markets see a prolonged squeeze, as Chinese [export restrictions](https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/china-restricts-fertiliser-exports-further-crimping-war-tightened-supply-2026-03-19/) coincide with war-related disruptions to sulphur and ​ammonia feedstocks.

In the end, some growers may just "roll the dice" and reduce fertiliser applications, putting yields at risk, said Andy Jung of U.S. fertiliser group Mosaic [(MOS.N), opens new tab](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/MOS.N).

At least 2 million metric tons of urea production - equivalent to some 3% ​of annual seaborne trade - have been lost since the conflict began, according to Sarah Marlow of commodity data provider Argus, as plants shut down in the Middle East as well as in India, Bangladesh and Russia.

Nearly 1 million tons already loaded on vessels, ‌meanwhile, remain stuck ⁠in the Gulf.

Even if hostilities end soon and the Hormuz strait reopens, just clearing the queue will take weeks, said Mark Milam of commodity market intelligence firm ICIS.

And fertiliser availability will likely remain constrained for months due to damage to Gulf production facilities and competition for limited alternative supplies.

"It's going to take a while to get back to normal," said Stephen Nicholson, Rabobank's head of North American grains and oilseeds.

## GLOBAL FOOD PRODUCTION AT RISK

Many farms still have fertilisers on hand, while record harvests last year have boosted global grain stocks. So the immediate impact of the current crisis on global food supplies may be limited.

Agricultural bodies, including the International Grains Council, are already cutting their forecasts for the next harvests, ​however. And the United Nations, which is trying to ​negotiate [shipping access for fertiliser](https://www.reuters.com/world/diplomatic-push-underway-hormuz-fertiliser-proposal-un-says-shortages-bite-2026-04-13/) through the Gulf, has sounded ⁠the alarm over food security in [developing nations](https://www.reuters.com/world/fertiliser-shortages-due-iran-war-are-key-worry-developing-world-un-agency-says-2026-04-14/).

In 2022, high [fertiliser costs](https://www.reuters.com/markets/europe/fertiliser-costs-could-prolong-global-food-tensions-fao-2022-06-09/) contributed to exacerbated hunger in poor, import-dependent countries, and analysts say regions like East Africa are again vulnerable.

Australia may offer an early indication of the impact on production of global staples.

In the bread-basket state of [Western Australia](https://www.reuters.com/business/western-australia-farmers-turn-canola-over-wheat-fuel-fertiliser-costs-rise-2026-04-17/), one industry group now expects the wheat planting area to drop by 14% ​as growers shift away from the fertiliser-intensive, low-margin grain.

Farmers still growing wheat may simply cut fertiliser application rates.

"If we see a drop-off in application in Australia and ​we start seeing expected yields come ⁠down, it could be quite an ominous sign for what's in store for everybody else," said Matthew Biggin, senior commodities analyst at BMI.

In Brazil, the world's biggest soybean exporter, analysts also expect farmers to use less fertiliser and potentially switch to [cheaper, less effective products](https://www.reuters.com/business/brazil-sounds-alarm-fertilizers-price-spike-spurs-cheaper-alternatives-2026-03-18/) like ammonium sulphate.

Yields for Southeast Asian palm oil - the world's most produced edible oil, already facing tight supply - could also fall, while Amit Guha, an independent Kuala Lumpur-based agronomist, warned that nutrient shortfalls posed longer-term risks to younger trees.

In Europe, ⁠spring planting decisions ​are shifting against input-intensive corn in countries including France, while reduced top-up nitrogen applications may trim protein content in this summer's wheat harvest, analysts ​said.

The larger risk, however, will come during autumn planting, when cash-strapped European farmers could cut overall grain area.

"That's why we're starting to get a little worried about the 2027 harvest," said Benoit Fayaud of Expana.

Reporting by Gus Trompiz in Paris, Naveen ​Thukral in Singapore, Ed White in Winnipeg, May Angel in London, Oliver Griffin in Sao Paulo and Mirac Dereli in Istanbul; Additional reporting by Sybille de la Hamaide and Tristan Veyet in Paris; Editing by Simon Webb and Joe Bavier

- Summary

- Activists urge Western Balkans to prioritize renewables, citing climate and economic risks of gas
- U.S. company AAFS to build gas pipeline after Bosnia-Croatia agreement, investing 1.5 billion euros
- EU delegation urges Bosnia to align energy ​policy with EU standards and focus on renewables

SARAJEVO, April 27 (Reuters) - Environmental activists in ‌the Western Balkans on Monday urged their governments to resist lobbying from the United States to build gas infrastructure in the region, and focus on renewable energy, in line with EU policy.

As the European Union plans to ban all Russian ​gas imports by 2028, the U.S. wants to pump its excess gas and oil into ​the Western Balkans and cut the region's dependence on Russian gas and oil. Activists ⁠say new gas projects would have a negative impact on climate and on the economy, arguing ​long-term investments will not pay off.

Make sense of the latest ESG trends affecting companies and governments with the Reuters Sustainable Switch newsletter. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-sustainable-switch/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fsustainability%2Fboards-policy-regulation%2Fenvironmentalists-western-balkans-call-renewables-over-us-gas-projects-2026-04-27%2F)

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Bosnia and Herzegovina (BiH) and Croatia are due to sign an inter-governmental agreement at ​the Three Sea Initiative summit in Dubrovnik later this week on transporting U.S. natural gas to Bosnia from a liquefied natural gas terminal on the Croatian island of Krk.

The agreement will authorise U.S. company AAFS Infrastructure and Energy LLC ​to build the Southern Interconnection gas pipeline and run it under a concession agreement.

"Permitting, expropriation and construction ​could easily take a decade and the infrastructure alone would cost billions, even before the costs of gas are included," ‌activists ⁠from 47 non-governmental organisations said in a joint statement.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

They appealed to the governments of Albania, Bosnia, Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia and Kosovo to autonomously decide on their energy plans, in line with EU legislation and policy and based on updated and publicly available evidence and public consultations.

AAFS Infrastructure and Energy LLC, formed ​last year in the United ​States and run by ⁠Jesse Binnall, a former Trump lawyer, and Joseph Flynn, the brother of Trump’s former national security adviser Michael Flynn, has said it would invest about 1.5 ​billion euros ($1.76 billion) in the project.

Earlier this month, Bosnia's regional parliament [amended the ​law](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/bosnia-selects-us-investor-gas-link-with-croatia-cut-reliance-russia-2026-04-15/) regulating the ⁠gas pipeline project to name AAFS as its key investor and leader.

The EU delegation in Bosnia last week warned the country, which aspires to join the bloc, that it needs to align its legislation and standards with ⁠those ​of the EU, including on energy policy.

"Bosnia and Herzegovina has enormous ​renewable energy potential, and the focus of EU energy policy in BiH is to support the country to realise this potential," ​the EU delegation said in a statement to Reuters.

($1 = 0.8517 euros)

Reporting by Daria Sito-Sucic; Editing by Susan Fenton

news.markdown:

![Luojiashan tanker sits anchored in Muscat, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, in Muscat](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/AE3FSQ522NMDXKNI7EF7SPHT5Y.jpg?auth=54a380d462e4ddaa26a23dc57ad24c481dbd8a0df111e00e3618f5cb77980115&width=1920&quality=80)

Tankers anchored in Muscat, Oman, amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, March 7, 2026. REUTERS/Benoit Tessier [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/luojiashan-tanker-sits-anchored-in-muscat-as-iran-vows-to-close-the-strait-of-hormuz-in-muscat/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX01UMUVDTUlJNDZTSEtJSTNJNFpYRjIzQ1o1RjdHMzJMWlAw/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

April 27 - When the dates were set for two critical meetings on the energy transition, no one could have guessed that one of the world’s biggest energy shocks would already be changing the calculus. War in the Middle East has [disrupted oil and gas supplies](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/oil-jumps-more-than-2-us-iran-peace-talks-stall-2026-04-26/ "https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/oil-jumps-more-than-2-us-iran-peace-talks-stall-2026-04-26/") and sent oil prices spiking, making the case for the transition and forcing countries across the world to rethink their approach to energy security.

As the president of the Asian Development Bank Masato Kanda [remarked earlier this month](https://www.cfr.org/event/a-conversation-with-president-masato-kanda-of-the-asian-development-bank "https://www.cfr.org/event/a-conversation-with-president-masato-kanda-of-the-asian-development-bank"), “a crisis of this magnitude is also a catalyst”.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Will it then spur action at meetings 5,000 miles apart – in London and Santa Marta in Colombia, where countries are making decisions critical to the transition? In London, members of the International Maritime Organization (IMO) are ​working to rescue a framework to transition the global shipping industry from fossil fuels, which was derailed last year by U.S. pressure.

In Santa Marta, 57 countries have gathered to chart the policy levers and private sector actions that must be taken to phase out fossil fuels. Presently there are both ‌producers (such as Canada, Norway, Australia and Nigeria) alongside importers, including European Union member states, the United Kingdom, Cambodia and Sri Lanka. Their efforts will feed into the work of the Brazilian government to develop a roadmap for the transition, to be launched later this year at the COP31 climate conference in Turkey.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Alongside them are sub-national governments, climate scientists, civil society and business, all aligned on the need to phase out fossil fuels. They are not expected to solve every problem, but to use this summit as a first step to build momentum for the transition. Science experts have helped Colombia to develop the first roadmap to get off fossil fuels, while reaping big long-term economic benefits.

Briefing journalists, Katrine Petersen, who works on clean power diplomacy at think-tank E3G, pointed to the significance of so many countries showing up in Santa Marta, “not to talk about whether there is a transition and whether ​it should happen, but how to move it along and how to make it equitable and orderly”.

![Oil pumpjacks are pictured in an Ecopetrol oil field in Barrancabermeja](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/ZB4X6PMXZJO5NHA754FPSO7JWU.jpg?auth=f64760319356549e7ff738260548cce8845c222defc8bcb9b14cc01a08481398&width=1920&quality=80)

Oil pumpjacks are pictured in an Ecopetrol oil field in Barrancabermeja, Colombia. The country's decision to stop oil exploration led to it being downgraded by international ratings agencies. REUTERS/Nelson Bocanegra [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/oil-pumpjacks-are-pictured-in-an-ecopetrol-oil-field-in-barrancabermeja/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX01UMUVDTUlJNDZTSEtJSTNJNFpYRjIzQ1o1RjdHMzJMWlAx/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

The summit has three strands. The first is to address the cost of capital for clean electricity infrastructure and the trap many countries of the Global South face in relying on extracting and exporting fossil fuels to service their debt ​and maintain public expenditure.

One factor in Colombia’s downgrading by international ratings agencies over the past few years was its decision in 2023 to stop new exploration for fossil fuels, which has made it harder for the country to pay for debt.

“Supporting countries in attracting the ⁠capital to do what they need to do to make the transition happen is critically important,” observes Jules Kortenhorst, co-chair of the Energy Transitions Commission. “There is (financial) innovation happening, but it's not happening fast enough.”

The Santa Marta meeting will also have to address the thorny question of supply and demand, which raises nuanced questions of what countries do with existing supplies, or even ​newly discovered resources.

“The real challenge with a phase-out plan for fossil fuels is that you can only phase out supply alongside phasing out demand,” adds Kortenhorst.

He says the efficiency of electric systems, which can deliver the useful energy of fossil fuels, means “a very large part of fossil fuel demand can already be cost-effectively phased out as of today, as a result of clean ​electrification”.

“The most important thing to come out of Santa Marta is specific action plans related to clean electrification,” and on how to address the current price premium of decarbonisation options in hard-to-abate sectors.

“If we can get more countries to understand that today, without any question, in most of the world, solar plus battery storage is the lowest-cost form f power-generation,” he says. At the same time, countries should focus on bringing in policies to reduce demand.

That power shift is happening. [Analysis from energy think-tank Ember](https://ember-energy.org/app/uploads/2026/04/Global-Electricity-Review-2026.pdf "https://ember-energy.org/app/uploads/2026/04/Global-Electricity-Review-2026.pdf") shows clean power covered all the growth in energy demand in 2025, leading to a small drop in fossil generation. Last week, the UK’s energy secretary Ed Miliband said the government would speed up the drive for clean power: “For Britain and so many other countries, clean energy is now the only route to financial security, energy security and ​indeed national security.”

But the World Benchmarking Alliance, which is also providing input to the conference, finds that continued dependence on fossil fuels is not just a climate threat, but an economic one, as sectors vital to the economy fail to put transition plans in place.

It’s [recommending mandatory greenhouse gas emissions disclosures](https://www.worldbenchmarkingalliance.org/latest/advancing-corporate-transition-planning-enable-fossil-free-future "https://www.worldbenchmarkingalliance.org/latest/advancing-corporate-transition-planning-enable-fossil-free-future") especially for large companies, and timelines for the phase out of ​fossil fuels in energy-intensive sectors.

![Activists and Indigenous people take part in a Stop EACOP campaign protest against fossil fuels during the UN Climate Change Conference (COP30) in Belem](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/CZMN4RZ3C5GXDCJBI6DTXJ4LQA.JPG?auth=ef5e5622c26c21df77b6144e71c4efe1f4faf34730d670e90966ee28ea4bec5a&width=1920&quality=80)

Activists and Indigenous people take part in a Stop EACOP campaign protest against fossil fuels during the UN Climate Change Conference (COP30) in Belem, Brazil, November 13, 2025. REUTERS/Adriano Machado [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersagency.com/en/licensereuterscontent/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

The third pillar of the discussions is international cooperation and climate diplomacy. On the agenda here is the investor state-dispute settlement (ISDS) system, a topic that’s been almost entirely absent in climate discussions but is seen as a barrier to transition, says Eunjung Lee, an adviser on the clean economy at E3G.

ISDS allows foreign investors to sue governments that take action, such as climate measures, ‌which could impact their ⁠profits. Fossil fuel investors have been big users of the system, receiving more than $80 billion in settlements paid out from the public purse. The Netherlands, which is a co-host, is facing cases from Exxon and Shell. And [a new report](https://www.globaljustice.org.uk/resource/european-isds-scorecard/ "https://www.globaljustice.org.uk/resource/european-isds-scorecard/") ranks the UK as the worst of 30 European countries when it comes to sustaining the system.

In March, more than 200 economists and legal experts [sent a letter](https://www.bu.edu/gdp/2026/03/19/isds-letter/ "https://www.bu.edu/gdp/2026/03/19/isds-letter/") to the Colombian president urging him to use the conference to build a coalition of countries committed to dismantling ISDS.

“The first step is to recognise (at a political level) that ISDS is a threat to climate action and the fossil fuel phase-out,” says Lee. It’s a component of over 2,500 trade and investment treaties between countries, so some form of collective action is needed. “Consolidating awareness and creating political momentum is the first step to advance the agenda,” she adds.

![LNG tanker at a port in Yantai](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/G3KGQBTGNVNPRHRWTJTBB5HZLY.jpg?auth=d311e050eaf10e368c1c27d91ee67d19a97b6d924ab42fc8705f0a98abbe7998&width=1920&quality=80)

A drone view shows tugboats assisting a liquified natural gas (LNG) tanker to dock at a port in Yantai, Shandong province, China February 14, 2025. cnsphoto via REUTERS [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/lng-tanker-at-a-port-in-yantai/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjU6bmV3c21sX01UMUVDTUlJQk40T002Vlc2M1hXVldDN0xRSURSUlFHWlAx/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

While naysayers are absent in Colombia, they are present in London at the IMO talks. The U.S. has repeatedly made clear it does not want a price on shipping emissions.

Since its strong-arm diplomacy forced a year’s delay in voting on the IMO’s net-zero framework ( [read more](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/boards-policy-regulation/analysis-shipping-climate-plan-delay-could-sink-clean-fuel-projects--ecmii-2025-10-27/ "https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/boards-policy-regulation/analysis-shipping-climate-plan-delay-could-sink-clean-fuel-projects--ecmii-2025-10-27/")) the U.S. position ​has hardened further, including calls for the EU to close its emissions trading system. ​Since 2024, the ETS has required the largest ships to pay for their carbon ⁠emissions.

“There’s a lot at stake: it's about shipping but it's also about the energy transition, and it's about multilateralism,” observes Tristan Smith, who leads University College London’s Shipping and Oceans Research Group.

The IMO’s net-zero framework, a compromise that has been years in the making, would create a global fuel standard and a global price on shipping emissions. These are intended to incentivise international shipping to switch to cleaner fuels and to provide a fund to support developing nations in their transition, including compensating them for impacts such as increased food prices.

The framework as it ​stands would discourage the use of liquified natural gas (LNG), owing to methane emissions in production and the [escape of unburned methane](https://theicct.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/ICCT_Comer_COP27_methane_slip_mp4-1.pdf "https://theicct.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/ICCT_Comer_COP27_methane_slip_mp4-1.pdf") from ships’ engines. The United States wants LNG to be accepted as a transition fuel and has pressed for it on grounds of affordability for low-income ​states.

But events in the Strait of Hormuz have driven ⁠up oil and gas prices and [doubled the cost of shipping](https://www.transportenvironment.org/articles/shipping-at-risk-in-the-strait-of-hormuz "https://www.transportenvironment.org/articles/shipping-at-risk-in-the-strait-of-hormuz"), according to analysis by environmental group Transport & Environment. Smith point out that the price hike “was the best way to remind people that not only is LNG a fossil fuel… as an economic strategy, it's a really suicidal way to move the shipping industry.”

Argentina, Liberia and Panama have come in behind the U.S., with proposals to replace the net-zero framework and abandon plans for an IMO fund. The fund itself is key to support for the framework from Pacific Island states, who compromised in their call for a universal $150 levy on all emissions.

Vanuatu’s climate and energy minister, Ralph Regenvanu, has warned that any attempt to water down the framework will force them back to that original demand.

Last week saw technical discussions on ⁠key details of the framework, ​such as greenhouse gas pricing and the reward and subsidy mechanisms.

These work by charging ships that miss ratcheting climate targets and recycling the money into tradable credits and a central fund to reward first movers and mitigate ​impacts on developing nations.

These fine details of how the framework will operate in practice were missing last October when the vote to go-ahead with NZF was delayed.

“We saw a lot of countries, especially low-income countries, come in and support the (net zero) fund and reward mechanism, including very clear voices from Africa and a key group of Pacific countries,” says Smith.

Another important signal comes from China, which abstained in October on the vote to delay. China ​submitted a paper to the negotiations on how the reward mechanism would work, “a positive sign of their continued engagement in the development of greenhouse gas pricing” he adds.

This week is a crunch point for the shipping industry, and whether it can begin to turn its back on fossil fuels. The signal it sends will be heard not just in Santa Marta, but around the world.

news.title: China's clean tech exporters cash in as Iran war hits oil & gas flows
news.snippet: China's exporters of batteries, solar systems, electric vehicles and other clean energy components posted record sales last month as the Iran war and...
news.description: China's exporters of batteries, solar systems, electric vehicles and other clean energy components posted record sales last month as the Iran war and...
news.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/chinas-clean-tech-exporters-cash-iran-war-hits-oil-gas-flows-2026-04-27/#main-content)

![Workers inspect solar panels at a photovoltaic power station on a hill in Linyi](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/ASURN4HH6NBMDGGVL2GDIBWPZU.JPG?auth=5235a08c5fe264b7050c0eff3e786145ce181c769d14cae85709edbeb6efbd37&width=1920&quality=80)

Workers inspect solar panels at a photovoltaic power station on a hill in Linyi, Shandong province, China August 11, 2018. REUTERS/Stringer [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersagency.com/en/licensereuterscontent/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

LITTLETON, Colorado, April 27 (Reuters) - China's exporters of batteries, solar systems, electric vehicles and other clean energy components posted record sales last month as the Iran war and subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz cut off oil and gas supplies from the Middle East.

Combined sales of Chinese clean energy parts and products ​totalled $26 billion in March, data compiled by Ember shows, which was by far the highest clean tech monthly tally ever recorded by the world's top battery, ‌EV and solar panel maker.

The Reuters Power Up newsletter provides everything you need to know about the global energy industry. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-power-up/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fbusiness%2Fenergy%2Fchinas-clean-tech-exporters-cash-iran-war-hits-oil-gas-flows-2026-04-27%2F)

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Exports of batteries and solar sytems surged in March as the war with Iran scrambled global oil and gas markets and reinforced support for home-grown energy supplies

The monthly receipts total was up 30% from February and 52% above the same month in 2025 as the seismic shock of bombings across the Middle East and closure of key shipping lanes sparked a surge in orders for clean energy products across the world.

## BATTERY BOOM

Battery systems were by far China's top seller among the suite of clean energy components last month, with just over $10 billion in sales recorded.

Battery exports from China in March 2026 topped $10 billion for the first time, spurred on by worries about the Iran war's impact on oil & fuel supply

That ​compares to average monthly battery export receipts of around $7 billion since the start of 2025, and so marked a sharp acceleration in global orders for battery systems used ​by utilities for energy storage and in electric vehicles.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Europe was the top region for Chinese battery imports, accounting for 43% or $4.3 billion of the ⁠total, followed by Asia, which snapped up 29% of the export volumes.

At the country level, Germany was the top market for Chinese batteries in March ($1.26 billion), followed by the United States ($823 ​million), the Netherlands ($635 million), Vietnam ($597 million) and Australia ($595 million).

Germany also registered the largest monthly increase in battery imports compared to February, posting a $286 million increase in import purchases in March.

Vietnam and Oman also ​registered monthly increases in excess of $200 million last month.

## SOLAR SPURT

Chinese solar systems recorded the next-largest jump in export receipts in March, jumping from $2.1 billion in February to $4.8 billion, which was the highest monthly total since May 2023 and roughly twice the monthly average since 2025.

Solar system exports shot sharply higher in most regions in March as the war with Iran underscored the importance of home-grown energy supplies

Asia was the top destination, accounting for 43% or $2 billion of the total, followed by Europe, which snapped up 27% of the total, worth $1.3 billion.

The Netherlands was the ​top overall solar panel importer in March, paying $400 million, which marked a near $200 million increase from the month before and compares to a monthly average of around $264 million since the start ​of 2025.

Indonesia, the Philippines, India and Pakistan rounded out the five largest buyers of China's solar systems last month.

## EV EXPORT VOLATILITY

China's exports of electric vehicles have hit turbulence so far in 2026 as changes ‌to subsidy schemes ⁠in several countries impacted consumer demand even before the Middle East war disrupted global trade flows and consumer confidence.

Total EV exports during January to March hit a record $21 billion, despite a steep fall in sales to the Middle East in March due to the Iran war

Even so, total EV exports for the first three months of 2026 were just over $21 billion, which marked a record for those months and compared to just $12 billion during the same months in 2025.

Europe was by far the largest destination for Chinese EVs in March, accounting for 45% of the sales total, followed by Asia, which had a 25% share.

Belgium was the top country for EV exports, followed by Brazil, the United Kingdom, Germany and Australia.

One notable feature ​of EV export flows in March was the ​steep collapse in sales to the Middle ⁠East as the air raids in the region brought the movement of goods to a near standstill.

Indeed, the Middle East only accounted for 4% of China's EV exports in March, compared to an 11% share of EV exports in 2025.

Exports hit record highs in Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America & Oceania in March just as oil & gas flows from the Middle East were cut off

The Middle East also registered a steep decline ​in deliveries for Chinese-made grid systems in March for the same reasons, and reveals that China's exporters of clean energy components have ​suffered some setbacks from the ⁠Iran war.

However, those drops in Middle East deliveries also indicate potential pent-up demand once peace returns and trade flows finally resume into and out of the region.

That in turn puts Chinese clean tech exporters in a good position to sustain strong global sales of their products, even after flows of Middle Eastern oil, gas and fuel start to recover.

news.description: Australia's central bank injected just enough cash into the banking system on Monday to match estimated requirements but short-term rates remained high,...
news.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://www.reuters.com/article/business/credit-wrapup-1-australia-meets-cash-needs-cbankers-watchful-idUSSP169939/#main-content)

[Exclusive news, data and analytics for financial market professionalsLearn more aboutRefinitiv](https://www.reuters.com/differentiator/)

By Tony Munroe and Ian Chua

HONG KONG, Sept 10 (Reuters) - Australia's central bank injected just enough cash into the banking system on Monday to match estimated requirements but short-term rates remained high, as global central bankers kept an eye on tight credit conditions.

Get a daily digest of breaking business news straight to your inbox with the Reuters Business newsletter. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/business/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Farticle%2Fbusiness%2Fcredit-wrapup-1-australia-meets-cash-needs-cbankers-watchful-idUSSP169939%2F)

Central bankers gathered in the Swiss city of Basel for talks on the world economy said on Sunday a wait-and-see stance was the best approach for now, given market volatility. \[ID:nL06214343\]

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Last Thursday the Reserve Bank of Australia broadened the type of assets it accepts in repurchase agreements in a bid to calm worries about credit market liquidity that have roiled world markets since July after problems emerged with U.S. home loans.

The central bank did not inject funds on Friday because the system was awash with cash.

"This is a follow-up to what they announced last week to test the waters to see if things stabilise," said Stephen Roberts, chief economist at Grange Securities in Australia, after the announcement of Monday's liquidity injection.

"But clearly it hasn't, with bank bills still above 7 percent, and you have a steeply inverse yield curve. One can't read much into today's action, but if such tight conditions prevail, then the (official) cash rate would have to be cut as a measure of last resort."

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Central banks in Britain, Europe and the United States have taken extraordinary steps to provide liquidity in the last few weeks.

Late on Friday, India's central bank said it would be responsive to any rise in global uncertainties that could threaten growth and stability in the economy.

"Monetary policy in India would continue to be vigilant and proactive in the context of any accentuation of global uncertainties that pose threats to growth and stability in the domestic economy," Governor Yaga Venugopal Reddy said.

A double dose of bad economic news triggered renewed worries about the health of the U.S. and Japanese economies, and the U.S. data fuelled expectations that the Federal Reserve would cut interest rates by 50 basis points next week.

"A September Fed rate cut is a done deal," said Hiroshi Yoshida, a forex trader at Shinkin Central Bank. "The market now focuses on whether it is by 25 basis points or 50 basis points."

US, JAPAN STUTTER

On Monday, revised data showed Japan's economy contracted a sharper-than-expected 0.3 percent in the second quarter, although some analysts said the country's growth remained broadly on track. \[ID:nT297235\]

That followed Friday's report showing U.S. payrolls shrank in August for the first time in four years. \[ID:nN07222995\]

"The U.S. jobs data and the GDP figures dealt a double whammy to the market, so we're seeing some panic buying," said Takafumi Yamawaki, a fixed-income strategist at Morgan Stanley in Tokyo, as prices of Japanese and U.S. government bonds rose.

Reflecting expectations of a hefty U.S. rate cut in the near term, the two-year U.S. Treasury yield fell  1 basis point to 3.90 percent and was 135 basis points below the 5.25 percent fed funds rate, holding near the biggest such gap since December 2000.

The two-year yield  on Japanese government bonds (JGBs) sank 4-½ basis points, hitting 0.77 percent, its lowest since mid-March.

Yield spreads between emerging market debt and U.S. Treasury notes <11EMJ> -- a key gauge of risk aversion -- widened by 3 basis points after blowing out by 8 bps on Friday.

Worries about the U.S. economy sent the dollar <.DXY> to a 15-year low against a basket of currencies.

While the Japanese GDP number disappointed, Finance Minister Fukushiro Nukaga said he expected the economy to continue recovering and did not think Japan would be hit hard by the U.S. subprime mortgage crisis.

Swap contracts on the Japanese overnight call rate  now suggest a less than 10 percent chance of a Bank of Japan rate rise to 0.75 percent on Sept. 19, while the possibility of an increase in December is less than 40 percent.

Before the subprime problems, the BOJ had been expected to raise rates in August, but it held fire.

Asian stock markets fell across the board on Monday as fears of a U.S. recession took its toll on shares in exporters. The MSCI index of Asian stocks excluding Japan <.MIAPJ0000PUS> was down 1.3 percent and Japan's Nikkei <.N225> dropped 2.22 percent.

news.title: Timely action on U.S. rescue critical: BIS official
news.snippet: The new U.S. financial landscape heralds some changes for the better but speedy action on the proposed bailout for troubled banks is critical,...
news.description: The new U.S. financial landscape heralds some changes for the better but speedy action on the proposed bailout for troubled banks is critical,...
news.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://www.reuters.com/article/world/timely-action-on-us-rescue-critical-bis-official-idUSTRE48N6RR/#main-content)

[Exclusive news, data and analytics for financial market professionalsLearn more aboutRefinitiv](https://www.reuters.com/differentiator/)

By Krista Hughes

BASEL (Reuters) - The new U.S. financial landscape heralds some changes for the better but speedy action on the proposed bailout for troubled banks is critical, a top Bank for International Settlements official said on Wednesday.

In an interview with Reuters, BIS chief economist Stephen Cecchetti said central bank interest rates and liquidity injections were only of limited help in combating the turmoil which has plagued financial markets since August last year.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

The United States' $700 billion plan to buy up toxic assets took the right approach in tackling the underlying issue of financial institutions' solvency but details of the rescue effort and its implementation were still unclear.

"The timely implementation of a rescue plan is critical," Cecchetti said in his office at the Swiss-based BIS, which acts as a forum for the world's central banks.

"Officials in the United States understand that every day, every hour that goes by is jeopardizing their ability to insulate the real economy from these problems."

U.S. Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke and Treasury Secretary Henry Paulson have both urged Congress to approve the plan, warning of the risk to the economy from market turmoil.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

The move follows the U.S. government's takeover of mortgage companies Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and bailout of insurer American International Group Inc, the bankruptcy of investment bank Lehman Brothers Holdings Inc and the conversion of rivals Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley into bank holding companies.

Cecchetti, who joined the BIS in July from the United States' Brandeis University and also worked at the New York Fed, said there appeared little need for other countries to enact similar rescue plans, since their financial institutions appeared to be in sounder shape.

"There have been concerns in the UK as well but ... Japanese and European institutions have been on the buying side of these assets, not on the selling side, so the implication is that they have the capital to do this," he said.

"Given that, it suggests that there's no real call for action outside those places."

In the United States, the demise of the separate investment banking model was positive in that it would change the nature of capital buffers, give institutions access to short-term central bank liquidity and bring them under the regulatory and supervisory umbrella, he said.

RATE CUTS NO HELP

Cecchetti said he did not think that interest rate cuts at major central banks would help to calm the situation, and continued tensions in money markets showed that flooding markets with liquidity had not helped much either.

Benchmark Euribor interest rates are at historic highs from one week to six months and ECB figures show some banks prefer to deposit excess cash at the central bank overnight rather than lend it on the market.

"We are in a position right now where interest rates are really not enough," he said.

"They might be able to help very slightly, but if I want to right the U.S. financial system today and I am given the choice between a 100 basis point cut in the Fed funds rate or a several hundred billion dollar program designed to shore up the capital positions of American financial intermediaries, I would pick the second one in an instant. It is clear that the Federal Reserve is doing the same."

NEW WORLD?

More fundamentally, policymakers were wrestling with the question of whether the financial system was entering a new era, potentially marked by the higher cost of capital and higher spreads between wholesale money market rates and official rates.

Banks' desire to hold more liquid assets meant demand for central bank money could be permanently higher going forward, the weighting of the world economy would shift toward Asia and financial supervision and regulation would improve, although policymakers would never stamp out risk-taking.

"Will there be less risk-taking per se? You are not going to stop risk-taking, there are always some risk-takers out there, many people are by their very nature are risk-takers. There will always be risk-taking, but it will become more expensive."

Cecchetti said he hoped crackdowns on market practices such as short-selling -- which has been curbed in countries including the United States, Britain and Australia -- would prove temporary, although predatory behavior could pose problems.

Short sellers borrow shares and sell them on in the hope of buying them back at a lower price to make a profit.

The BIS is a member of the global Financial Stability Forum, which is meeting next week to discuss progress on its financial reform agenda ahead of a Group of Seven meeting in October.

Cecchetti said key aims were to ensure that financial institutions managed their liquidity needs adequately and understand the feedback loop between the financial sector and the real economy.

"The financial sector is one of the most regulated sectors in the economy, certainly on a par with the control that we have over pharmaceuticals, and we want to make sure that this self-reinforcing cycle is not exacerbated by the way we design our regulatory and supervisory frameworks," he said.

"I would much rather have thoughtful implementation of changes in the financial structure than emergency, ill-thought-out ones. There's a tremendous amount of work going on right now to ensure that our policy recommendations and implementation do not have unintended consequences."

"Rushing is never a good idea. But sometimes you are left without any alternative."

Editing by Ron Askew

- Summary
- Companies

- Provincial, federal government near agreement on carbon pricing for oil sands companies
- Broader agreement on a new pipeline, carbon-capture project remains elusive
- Alberta aims to present pipeline proposal by July 1 despite unresolved cost-sharing for Pathways project
- Carney has said pipeline plan cannot proceed without oil sands commitment to Pathways

CALGARY, April 27 (Reuters) - Canada and Alberta are expected to strike a deal in the next two weeks that will increase the price on carbon for ‌the province's industrial emitters, two sources with knowledge of the talks said, but a broader agreement to tackle oil sands greenhouse gases and green-light a new crude oil export pipeline remains elusive.

Canada's federal government and its main oil-producing province have been in talks since November, when both parties [agreed](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/climate-energy/canada-drops-emissions-cap-oil-gas-sector-agreement-with-alberta-2025-11-27/) to work together to boost investment in energy production.

Make sense of the latest ESG trends affecting companies and governments with the Reuters Sustainable Switch newsletter. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-sustainable-switch/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fsustainability%2Fclimate-energy%2Fcanada-alberta-close-carbon-price-agreement-sources-say-2026-04-27%2F)

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Prime Minister Mark Carney ​has rolled back climate rules in order to help the oil-and-gas sector as part of an effort to make Canada's economy more resilient against ​U.S. President Donald Trump's [tariffs](https://www.reuters.com/business/tariffs/). He has said he is willing to get behind the construction of a new oil ⁠pipeline to the West Coast if Alberta agrees to strengthen its pollution pricing scheme and if Canada's biggest oil sands companies will sign on to the ​giant C$16.5 billion ($12.1 billion) Pathways carbon capture and storage project.

But getting all of those moving parts to snap into place that address Canada's twin goals of boosting ​economic growth and tackling climate change is proving to be thorny, and it is unclear whether a pricing agreement will increase the chances that the other negotiations are ultimately successful. The three parties recently missed a self-imposed April 1 deadline on divvying up the costs of the larger carbon-capture project.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

The two sources said a carbon pricing agreement that would increase ​the effective price the province's heavy emitters must pay on carbon to C$130 a metric ton is very close to being finalized. Alberta froze that ​price in May of last year. Credits currently trade between C$20 and C$40 a metric ton - too low to incentivize polluters to invest in emissions reduction technology, experts said.

The sources ‌did not ⁠say what year the deal will require the target to be reached.

## GREENHOUSE GASES AND PIPELINES

Experts say a carbon pricing deal is necessary first to make the economics of the Pathways project work. Pathways, first proposed by the companies in 2022, would be one of the world's largest such projects, with the potential to significantly reduce emissions from the oil sands, Canada's largest source of greenhouse gases.

At the same time, Canada's oil industry is keen to grow production, and Alberta ​is working on a proposal for a ​new one-million-barrel-per-day [crude oil pipeline](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/climate-energy/ceraweek-canadas-pipeline-plan-attracts-middle-eastern-asian-interest-alberta-2026-03-23/) to British ⁠Columbia's northwest coast, hoping to entice a private company to build the line.

An Alberta government source said the province remains committed to presenting its pipeline proposal by July 1, and wants an agreement before then with Canada's oil sands ​companies that commits them to building the carbon capture and storage project. Carney has said any pipeline project must ​be built in ⁠tandem with that project.

Critics said all of the interlocking pieces must come together in order for Canada to meet its twin goals of climate action and economic growth, which is why it is concerning that the April 1 deadline has come and gone with only partial progress.

"If they (the oil sands) don't move forward with Pathways now, ⁠there is ​no way a pipeline could move ahead concurrently," said Janetta McKenzie, director of oil and gas ​at the Pembina Institute, a clean energy think-tank.

Representatives for both Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and the Oil Sands Alliance, which represents the five largest Canadian oil sands companies, said negotiations are ongoing. ​The prime minister's office did not respond to a request for comment.

(1 Canadian dollar = $0.73 U.S.)

Reporting by Amanda Stephenson in Calgary; Editing by Caroline Stauffer and David Gaffen
